content.text: Coordinating GenAI work across multiple Agile Release Trains (ARTs) introduces a unique set of challenges: shared model serving infrastructure, cross-ART data dependencies, synchronised evaluation cycles, and shared AI governance obligations. Effective cross-ART coordination for GenAI programs requires ceremonies, roles, and artefacts beyond those needed for traditional software ARTs.
What It Is

An Agile Release Train is the SAFe construct for organising 50-125 people into a self-managing, cross-functional delivery team that operates on a shared PI cadence. When a GenAI program spans multiple ARTs — for example, a Customer AI ART, an Operations AI ART, and a shared AI Platform ART — cross-ART coordination is required to manage shared dependencies, shared infrastructure, and shared governance.

Cross-ART coordination in SAFe happens through the Solution Train (if SAFe's Large Solution configuration is used), through ART Sync ceremonies (RTEs meeting weekly), and through shared PI Planning events where all ARTs plan simultaneously. For GenAI, additional coordination mechanisms are needed: a shared model registry, a cross-ART evaluation cadence, a shared AI risk register, and explicit ownership of the AI Platform ART that provides serving, MLOps tooling, and evaluation infrastructure as a shared service.
Why It Matters

GenAI programs that scale beyond a single ART frequently create an AI platform problem: multiple ARTs independently building model serving, evaluation pipelines, and training infrastructure, producing duplicated and incompatible tooling. The Customer AI ART uses MLflow; the Ops AI ART uses Weights & Biases; neither can share models or evaluations because their registries are incompatible. A shared AI Platform ART that provides these services as a platform to all feature ARTs solves the duplication problem, but only if the platform ART's roadmap is coordinated with the feature ARTs' needs.

Cross-ART data dependencies are equally problematic: a Customer AI ART's training pipeline may depend on a data asset owned by the Ops AI ART's data team. Without cross-ART dependency visibility in PI Planning, this dependency is discovered mid-sprint when the ML team is blocked, and resolving it requires an unplanned escalation that takes 2-5 business days. ART Sync ceremonies and cross-ART program boards are the structural solution.
Benefits
Shared AI infrastructure: a Platform ART providing model serving, evaluation, and MLOps tooling to all feature ARTs eliminates duplicated investment and incompatible tooling.
Cross-ART dependency visibility: ART Sync ceremonies surface data, model, and infrastructure dependencies between ARTs before they become blockers.
Consistent governance: a cross-ART AI risk register and shared evaluation standards ensure consistent quality and compliance posture across all GenAI systems in the portfolio.
Economies of scale: GPU infrastructure, foundation model API contracts, and evaluation framework investment shared across ARTs reduces per-ART cost.
Talent sharing: GenAI specialists (ML researchers, data scientists, AI safety engineers) can be organised in a Centre of Excellence that serves multiple ARTs without duplicating scarce talent.
Aligned PI cadence: all ARTs operating on the same PI calendar can hold a single Solution Demo and Inspect & Adapt event, reducing overhead and enabling cross-ART learning.
Trade-offs
Platform ART bottleneck: a shared Platform ART creates a single dependency point — if the Platform ART is under-resourced, all feature ARTs are blocked.
Cross-ART governance overhead: ART Sync ceremonies, cross-ART PI Planning, and Solution Demos add coordination overhead that may not be justified for smaller portfolios.
Architectural coupling: features ARTs that depend on the Platform ART's model serving layer are coupled to the Platform ART's release cadence.
Shared PI calendar constraint: requiring all ARTs to align on the same PI calendar reduces the flexibility for individual ARTs to optimise their own cadence.
How It Works
The AI Platform ART Pattern

The most important structural decision for multi-ART GenAI programs is whether to create a dedicated AI Platform ART or distribute platform capabilities across feature ARTs. The Platform ART pattern is appropriate when: two or more feature ARTs need model serving infrastructure; evaluation framework design requires specialist ML engineering expertise that should not be duplicated; or GPU infrastructure management is complex enough to warrant dedicated DevOps/MLOps teams. The Platform ART provides its capabilities as internal services consumed by feature ARTs through a published interface — the model serving API, the evaluation framework configuration, the MLOps pipeline template.
ART Sync Ceremony

The ART Sync is a weekly 30-minute meeting attended by the Release Train Engineers (RTEs) of all ARTs (and optionally Scrum Masters and team representatives). Its agenda: cross-ART dependency status updates (which cross-ART dependencies are at risk?); Platform ART capacity and bottleneck review (are feature ARTs' requests to the Platform ART feasible?); escalated impediments that individual ARTs could not resolve; and any cross-ART risks that need visibility. The ART Sync is the operational heartbeat of cross-ART coordination — it is not a status meeting but a dependency unblocking session.
Cross-ART Program Board

The cross-ART program board extends the standard SAFe program board to show all three ARTs' features and milestones on a single view, with cross-ART dependency arrows showing where Feature ART 1 depends on Platform ART deliverables or where Feature ART 1 depends on data from Feature ART 2. This board is maintained by the RTEs and reviewed at every ART Sync. For GenAI, the cross-ART board should prominently show model evaluation milestones, shared training data pipeline deliverables, and GPU capacity allocation by sprint.
Shared Model Registry

A shared model registry — maintained by the Platform ART but readable by all ARTs — is the coordination artefact that enables cross-ART model reuse and prevents divergent versioning. Each registered model has a version number, a model card, evaluation results, deployment status (staging / production), and a model owner. Feature ARTs consume registered models from the shared registry rather than maintaining private model versions; this means that a model improvement by the Customer AI ART's ML team can be reused by the Ops AI ART without retraining.
Cross-ART Solution Demo

At the end of each PI, a Solution Demo brings all ARTs together to demonstrate the integrated increment — not just individual ART features, but the end-to-end system as experienced by a user interacting with both the Customer AI and Ops AI systems. For GenAI, the Solution Demo should include a model quality summary section where each ART's ML lead presents the evaluation results for the PI's model versions. This creates accountability for model quality at the cross-ART level, not just at the individual team level.
Inspect and Adapt

Inspect and Adapt (I&A) is the retrospective and problem-solving session at the end of each PI. For a multi-ART GenAI program, I&A should explicitly include retrospective topics specific to AI work: cross-ART data dependency failures, evaluation pipeline reliability, model quality against PI Objective targets, and AI governance process friction. The problem-solving workshop in I&A should produce improvement stories that address systemic cross-ART issues — these are added to the Platform ART's next-PI backlog as enabler features.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Multi-ART GenAI coordination model showing Customer AI ART, Ops AI ART, and shared AI Platform ART with ART Sync and Solution Demo coordination ceremonies

content.text: When to Use It
When two or more ARTs share model serving infrastructure, training compute, or evaluation pipelines.
When GenAI workstreams in different ARTs have cross-ART data dependencies that are creating mid-sprint blockers.
When duplicated MLOps tooling across ARTs is creating incompatibility, cost duplication, or talent fragmentation.
When AI governance (risk assessment, safety evaluation, compliance) applies across multiple ARTs and requires a consistent approach.
When the GenAI portfolio has grown to the point where a single ART cannot deliver all planned initiatives within acceptable PI timeframes.
How to Implement
Audit existing ARTs for duplicated GenAI infrastructure investment — identify what could be consolidated into a Platform ART before creating one.
Define the Platform ART's service catalogue: what does it provide (model serving, eval framework, MLOps pipelines), and what does it not provide (feature-specific models, product UX)?
Align all ARTs on a shared PI calendar before the first cross-ART PI Planning event — shared cadence is the prerequisite for all other coordination.
Create the cross-ART program board before PI Planning and train all RTEs on maintaining cross-ART dependency arrows.
Launch the ART Sync ceremony as a standing weekly 30-minute meeting before it is needed — attempting to start it during a cross-ART crisis is too late.
Define Platform ART SLAs for shared services: what response time can feature ARTs expect for model deployment requests, evaluation framework updates, and GPU provisioning?
Include cross-ART model quality review as a standing section in the Solution Demo agenda — it should never be optional.
Examples
ART Sync meeting template

# ART Sync — Weekly | 30 min
# Date: {date} | Facilitator: Portfolio RTE | ARTs: Customer AI, Ops AI, Platform AI

## 1. Cross-ART Dependency Status (10 min)
| From ART     | To ART      | Dependency                              | Due Sprint | Status      |
|--------------|-------------|----------------------------------------|------------|-------------|
| Customer AI  | Platform AI | Model serving v3 API stable             | S3         | ON TRACK    |
| Ops AI       | Customer AI | Shared entity resolution service        | S4         | AT RISK     |
| Customer AI  | Ops AI      | Annotated complaint corpus (5k samples) | S2         | DONE        |

## 2. Platform ART Capacity (5 min)
- GPU cluster utilisation this sprint: 78%
- Pending requests from feature ARTs: 2 (Customer AI training run S3, Ops AI red-team eval S3)
- Capacity constraint: A100 cluster fully booked S3 — Ops AI training run moved to S4 (approved)

## 3. Escalated Impediments (10 min)
| Impediment                                     | Raised By    | Owner         | ETA     |
|------------------------------------------------|-------------|---------------|---------|
| Data DPA blocker on EU customer corpus         | Customer AI | Legal (PM)    | Jul 28  |
| Model serving v3 breaking change in API schema | Platform AI | Arch council  | Jul 25  |

## 4. Cross-ART Risks (5 min)
- R-07: Regulatory guidance on EU AI Act article 13 expected Aug 5 — may affect both ARTs
  Action: Platform ART + Legal to prepare impact assessment by Aug 4

## Next ART Sync: {next_date}
Cross-ART model registry schema (Python)

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class DeploymentStatus(Enum):
    EXPERIMENT = "experiment"
    STAGING = "staging"
    PRODUCTION = "production"
    DEPRECATED = "deprecated"

@dataclass
class EvalResult:
    eval_set: str
    metric: str
    value: float
    threshold: float
    passed: bool

    def summary(self) -> str:
        status = "PASS" if self.passed else "FAIL"
        return f"{self.metric}: {self.value:.3f} (threshold: {self.threshold:.3f}) [{status}]"

@dataclass
class ModelCard:
    model_id: str
    version: str
    owner_art: str
    owner_team: str
    base_model: str
    training_data_hash: str
    trained_date: date
    eval_results: list[EvalResult] = field(default_factory=list)
    status: DeploymentStatus = DeploymentStatus.EXPERIMENT
    consuming_arts: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    known_limitations: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    go_no_go: Optional[str] = None  # "GO" | "NO-GO" | "PENDING"

    def all_evals_passed(self) -> bool:
        return all(r.passed for r in self.eval_results)

    def promote_to_staging(self, approver: str) -> bool:
        if not self.all_evals_passed():
            print(f"Cannot promote {self.model_id}:{self.version} — eval failures")
            return False
        self.status = DeploymentStatus.STAGING
        self.go_no_go = "GO"
        print(f"Promoted {self.model_id}:{self.version} to STAGING (approved by {approver})")
        return True

    def summary(self) -> str:
        eval_summary = "; ".join(r.summary() for r in self.eval_results)
        consuming = ", ".join(self.consuming_arts) if self.consuming_arts else "none"
        return (f"[{self.status.value.upper()}] {self.model_id} v{self.version}
"
                f"  Owner: {self.owner_art} / {self.owner_team}
"
                f"  Base: {self.base_model} | Trained: {self.trained_date}
"
                f"  Evals: {eval_summary}
"
                f"  Consuming ARTs: {consuming}")

# Example shared model registry usage
registry: list[ModelCard] = []

customer_model = ModelCard(
    model_id="cust-intent-v2",
    version="2.4.1",
    owner_art="Customer AI ART",
    owner_team="ML Team A",
    base_model="llama-3-8b-instruct",
    training_data_hash="sha256:a1b2c3d4",
    trained_date=date(2026, 7, 15),
    eval_results=[
        EvalResult("intent-eval-v7", "precision", 0.891, 0.88, True),
        EvalResult("intent-eval-v7", "recall", 0.872, 0.85, True),
        EvalResult("safety-eval-v3", "policy_violation_rate", 0.008, 0.01, True),
    ],
    consuming_arts=["Customer AI ART", "Ops AI ART"],
    go_no_go="PENDING"
)

registry.append(customer_model)
customer_model.promote_to_staging(approver="ml_lead@co.com")
print()
print(customer_model.summary())
Real-World Example

A financial services organisation ran three GenAI ARTs — Customer AI ART (chatbot and self-service AI), Operations AI ART (fraud and claims AI), and a shared AI Platform ART (model serving, evaluation, MLOps). Before introducing an ART Sync ceremony and cross-ART program board, the RTEs discovered at the end of PI 2 that both feature ARTs had independently built incompatible evaluation frameworks — Customer AI using Promptfoo and Operations AI using LangSmith, with no shared schema for evaluation results. Model quality comparisons across ARTs were impossible, and evaluation infrastructure was duplicated. The Platform ART's PI 3 backlog was reoriented to deliver a unified evaluation framework as a shared service. Adoption took two PIs (20 weeks) but eliminated the duplication and made cross-ART model quality reporting feasible for the first time. The portfolio steering committee received a single cross-ART model quality dashboard from PI 5 onwards, replacing two incompatible reports that had previously made cross-program comparison impossible.
Common Mistakes
Creating a Platform ART without a service catalogue and SLAs — feature ARTs do not know what to expect and the Platform ART becomes a black-box dependency.
Running ART Syncs as status meetings rather than dependency unblocking sessions — if no actions come out of an ART Sync, it is not working.
Misaligned PI calendars across ARTs — feature ARTs and Platform ARTs on different sprint or PI cadences cannot coordinate dependencies effectively.

Allowing feature ARTs to build their own model serving or evaluation infrastructure in parallel with the Platform ART — this is the root cause of the duplication problem the Platform ART was created to solve.
Omitting model quality metrics from the Solution Demo — if the Solution Demo only shows software features, model quality improvements are invisible to stakeholders.
Not establishing a shared model registry before the first cross-ART model reuse need arises — retrofitting shared registry conventions after ARTs have built incompatible ones is extremely costly.
Best Practices
Define the Platform ART's service catalogue and SLAs before it is created — feature ARTs cannot plan around a shared service without knowing its capabilities and reliability.
Hold the ART Sync as a standing weekly 30-minute commitment — cancelling it under delivery pressure is when cross-ART coordination breaks down most catastrophically.
Include model quality summaries in the Solution Demo as a mandatory agenda item — make model quality a first-class citizen alongside product features.
Maintain the cross-ART program board continuously between PI Planning events — out-of-date dependency boards provide false confidence.
Establish a shared model registry before the first PI in which multiple ARTs might consume the same model.
Calibrate GPU capacity across the Platform ART and feature ARTs at PI Planning — training compute contention is the most common cross-ART bottleneck in GenAI programs.
Performance & Scalability

For portfolios with 4+ ARTs, the ART Sync attendee list grows unwieldy if all RTEs and all team representatives attend. Address this by structuring ART Sync as a two-tier model: a 15-minute RTE-only sync followed by a 15-minute expansion session where affected team representatives join for specific dependency discussions. Cross-ART dependency management tooling (Jira Align dependency views, digital program boards) reduces the time spent on status reporting in the ART Sync and allows more time for decision-making.

At 10+ ARTs, consider a Solution Train structure (SAFe Large Solution) with dedicated Solution Management and Solution Architecture roles. The Solution Train Engineer coordinates at a level above the ART, and Solution PI Planning replaces individual ART PI Planning. GenAI-specific Solution Train artefacts include a cross-ART model dependency map and a shared AI risk register maintained at the Solution level.
Tooling & Ecosystem
Jira Align: cross-ART program board, PI Planning board, dependency tracking, and ART Sync action tracking.
Miro / MURAL: cross-ART dependency map for PI Planning and ART Sync visual reference.
MLflow (centrally hosted): shared model registry for cross-ART model versioning and evaluation results.
DVC: cross-ART data pipeline versioning that enables tracking of which data version fed which model version.
Confluence: Platform ART service catalogue, SLAs, cross-ART evaluation standards, and AI risk register.
Grafana / Datadog: shared operational dashboards for cross-ART model performance monitoring — one view for all production GenAI systems.

Security & Reliability
A shared model registry must have role-based access: model cards readable by all ARTs; model promotion (staging, production) restricted to model owners and governance approvers.
Cross-ART dependencies on the Platform ART create a single point of failure — the Platform ART must have its own SRE runbook and incident response process.
GPU quota shared across ARTs must be governed to prevent one ART's training job from consuming all available compute and starving other ARTs — implement queue management and quota limits per ART.

Cross-ART model reuse introduces a transitive dependency: if ART A's model is consumed by ART B, a safety issue discovered in ART A's model affects ART B's production system. The cross-ART program board and ART Sync must have a protocol for broadcasting model safety issues to all consuming ARTs immediately.
The cross-ART AI risk register must be reviewed at every ART Sync — risks that apply to multiple ARTs are often underweighted when each ART manages only its own risk register.
Key Takeaways
Multi-ART GenAI programs require a dedicated AI Platform ART providing model serving, evaluation, and MLOps tooling as a shared service — without it, duplication and incompatibility are inevitable.
The ART Sync ceremony (weekly, 30 min, all RTEs) is the operational heartbeat of cross-ART coordination — treat it as a dependency unblocking session, not a status meeting.

The cross-ART program board must show model evaluation milestones, data pipeline deliverables, and GPU capacity allocation alongside product features — AI dependencies are invisible on a software-only program board.
A shared model registry — maintained by the Platform ART — is the artefact that enables cross-ART model reuse and consistent quality reporting.
Model quality summaries must be included in the Solution Demo alongside product feature demonstrations.
Misaligned PI calendars across ARTs make cross-ART dependency planning impossible — ensure all ARTs share the same PI start and end dates.
Cross-ART GPU quota contention is the most common operational bottleneck in multi-ART GenAI programs — manage it at the Platform ART level with explicit quota limits per feature ART.
